# **Advancements and Future Directions in the Profitable Tour Problem and its Multi-Period Variants**

## **1\. Introduction to Combinatorial Routing with Profits**

Combinatorial optimization within the domain of vehicle routing has increasingly shifted toward scenarios where visiting all nodes in a network is neither mandatory nor economically viable. The Profitable Tour Problem (PTP) represents a fundamental and exceptionally challenging class of these routing problems. At its core, the PTP mathematically formalizes the operational trade-off between the revenue generated by serving a specific customer and the logistical cost required to reach them. Unlike the standard Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) or the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), where a Hamiltonian cycle or a set of full-coverage routes must be constructed to visit every vertex exactly once, the PTP fundamentally requires the simultaneous resolution of two interconnected NP-hard decisions: the selection of an optimal subset of vertices to visit, and the determination of the optimal sequence in which to visit them. The objective is strictly defined as the maximization of the net profit, calculated as the difference between the total collected revenues and the total incurred travel costs.1

As global logistics networks, automated delivery systems, and tourism routing ecosystems evolve, static and deterministic single-period models fail to capture the operational complexities of modern supply chains. The routing research landscape has consequently expanded to encompass the Multi-Period Profitable Tour Problem (MPTP) and its periodic variants.3 These extensions introduce severe temporal dependencies, resource synchronizations (such as battery recharging for electric vehicles), and stochastic elements over extended planning horizons. In many practical situations, operating an unlimited fleet of capacitated vehicles to satisfy all demands is economically ruinous, necessitating selective routing frameworks like the PTP.4

This report provides an exhaustive, mathematically rigorous analysis of the state-of-the-art formulations, exact algorithms, matheuristics, and emerging Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) frameworks applied to the PTP and MPTP. Furthermore, it critically evaluates existing algorithmic gaps, audits the mathematical rigor of current formulations, and delineates the most promising strategic research directions for advancing theoretical bounds and practical scalability in top-tier optimization venues.

## **2\. Problem Taxonomy and The TSP-T3CO Scheme**

To establish theoretical rigor and audit the underlying assumptions of current methodologies, it is critical to differentiate the PTP from adjacent problems within the broader "TSP with Profits" family. A pervasive issue in routing literature is the inconsistent nomenclature and the conflation of fundamentally distinct objective functions. Recent literature employs the TSP-T3CO definition scheme to formally and precisely define TSP variants, revealing subtle structural differences and clarifying the approximability landscape of these models.2 Under this rigorous taxonomy, routing problems involving profits are classified based on how profits and costs are integrated into the mathematical optimization model.5

Table 1 delineates the precise distinctions between these variants, which is critical for understanding the valid inequalities and theoretical bounds applicable to each.

| Problem Variant | Objective Function Formulation | Primary Structural Constraints | Distinguishing Mathematical Characteristic |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Profitable Tour Problem (PTP)** | **![][image1]** | Degree constraints, Subtour elimination. | Both collected profit and incurred travel cost are integrated directly into the unconstrained objective function.5 |
| **Orienteering Problem (OP)** | **![][image2]** | **![][image3]** (Budget constraint). | Travel cost acts strictly as a hard resource budget constraint and is entirely unpenalized in the objective.6 |
| **Prize-Collecting TSP (PCTSP)** | **![][image4]** | **![][image5]** (Quota constraint). | Requires a minimum threshold of profit to be collected while minimizing costs and penalties for unvisited nodes.5 |

The foundation of the TSP-T3CO scheme allows for a concise and compact representation of approximability results, making open gaps in the theoretical bounds of these variants highly evident to researchers.2 The PTP on general metric graphs is strongly NP-hard and APX-hard. However, it is mathematically vital to note that the PTP is solvable in polynomial time on specific topological structures. For example, probabilistic PTPs where customers are distributed on trees can be solved optimally in ![][image6] time.7 Failing to recognize these topological advantages often leads researchers to deploy computationally heavy metaheuristics on graph structures that could be solved exactly via dynamic programming.

## **3\. Mathematical Formulations and Rigorous Audit**

To critically evaluate the work being proposed in top-tier AI and Operations Research venues, a rigorous formalization of the Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) models is required. The following sections formalize the base models and actively audit their assumptions.

### **3.1. The Standard Capacitated Profitable Tour Problem (CPTP)**

Let ![][image7] be a complete directed graph where ![][image8] is the set of vertices, with vertex ![][image9] representing the central depot, and ![][image10] representing the set of potential customers. Each arc ![][image11] incurs a routing cost ![][image12]. Each customer ![][image13] possesses a deterministic demand ![][image14] and a profit ![][image15] that is collected entirely upon visitation.1 A fleet of ![][image16] homogeneous vehicles, each with a maximum payload capacity ![][image17], is available at the depot.

The fundamental decision variables are defined as follows:

* ![][image18]: Equals ![][image19] if vehicle ![][image20] traverses arc ![][image21], and ![][image9] otherwise.
* ![][image22]: Equals ![][image19] if vehicle ![][image20] visits customer ![][image23], and ![][image9] otherwise.

The objective function maximizes the net profit 6:

![][image24]
This objective is subject to the following standard routing constraints:

![][image25]
![][image26]
![][image27]
![][image28]
![][image29]
![][image30]
**Critical Audit of the CPTP Formulation:**

While this standard formulation is ubiquitous in the literature , it contains significant vulnerabilities that must be addressed when targeting premier venues. First, the formulation inherently assumes the triangle inequality holds for costs ![][image12]. If the triangle inequality is violated (as is common in urban networks with one-way streets and varied elevation profiles), the model may artificially induce optimal solutions containing non-elementary loops if profits outstrip the negative cost cycles. Second, the Dantzig-Fulkerson-Johnson subtour elimination constraints presented above yield an exponentially large set of constraints (![][image31]), which renders the monolithic MILP mathematically intractable for any instance where ![][image32]. Implementing this without a delayed constraint generation (row generation) framework within a Branch-and-Cut solver is a methodological flaw. Finally, the assumption of strictly positive and deterministic demands ![][image14] is a severe relaxation of real-world stochasticity, limiting the model's empirical validity.

### **3.2. The Time-Dependent PTP with Resource Constraints (TDPTPRC)**

Real-world operations face road congestion, making static distance matrices obsolete. The TDPTPRC generalizes the PTP by defining travel times ![][image33] as non-linear functions of the specific departure time ![][image34].8 The objective function must evaluate the static profit against a travel cost measured fundamentally by the duration of the trip.9

If ![][image35] is defined as the continuous variable representing the departure time from node ![][image23], the time window constraints are formulated as ![][image36], where ![][image37] is the valid time window. The flow constraints are augmented by non-linear time-tracking constraints:

![][image38]
Where ![][image39] is a sufficiently large constant (Big-M).

**Critical Audit of the TDPTPRC Formulation:** The use of Big-M constraints for time-tracking is notoriously weak and heavily compromises the tightness of the LP relaxation. State-of-the-art exact methods attempt to linearize the time-dependent function by discretizing the planning horizon into time buckets or using piecewise linear approximations.10 However, discretization introduces a trade-off between the resolution of the time buckets and the exponential explosion of variables. A rigorous approach must utilize time-dependent valid inequalities. Recent literature has derived four new families of valid inequalities that exploit the mathematical properties of the travel time function (assuming the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) property holds) to tighten the standard formulation, allowing exact solvers to significantly reduce computation times on benchmark instances.8

### **3.3. The Multi-Period PTP with Electric Vehicles (MPTP-EV)**

The transition from single-period to multi-period models marks a massive escalation in computational complexity. A paramount advancement in this domain is the Multi-Period Profitable Tour Problem with Electric Vehicles and Mandatory Stops.3 Electric vehicles (EVs) suffer from limited range and prolonged recharging times, often creating costly idle times in standard daily routing. The MPTP-EV mitigates this by synchronizing battery charging operations with mandatory driver lunch breaks over a multi-period planning horizon.3 This necessitates solving a simultaneous location-routing problem to establish long-term agreements with a subset of restaurant clusters or charging stations.

Let ![][image40] be the set of periods (e.g., days in a planning horizon). The graph is extended to include a set of restaurant/charging nodes ![][image41]. The model introduces a binary decision variable ![][image42] which equals ![][image19] if the agreement with restaurant ![][image43] is activated during period ![][image44].

The objective function in the MPTP-EV integrates location and routing components:

![][image45]
where ![][image46] represents the amortized fixed cost of establishing a charging agreement at location ![][image47].

A critical vulnerability in current EV routing papers lies in the formulation of the State-of-Charge (SoC) tracking. Let ![][image48] represent the remaining battery level upon departing node ![][image23] in period ![][image34]. The constraints must enforce energy conservation:

![][image49]
Where ![][image50] is the energy consumed traversing arc ![][image21] and ![][image51] is the energy replenished at the mandatory stop.

**Critical Audit of MPTP-EV Assumptions:** Assuming that energy consumption ![][image50] is a static constant directly proportional to the physical distance ![][image12] is a pervasive mathematical flaw in urban logistics research.12 Energy consumption in an EV is highly non-linear; it is dependent on payload mass (which decreases as demands are delivered), road gradient, ambient temperature, and time-dependent traffic speeds. Models that fail to account for load-dependent energy consumption risk producing theoretically feasible routes that will definitively strand vehicles in practice. Literature indicates that up to 40 percent of infeasibilities induced by neglecting time dependency in EV routing can be caused by exceeding battery capacity.13 A scientifically grounded methodology targeting top-tier venues must model ![][image50] as a function of the vehicle's current load ![][image52] and speed, utilizing robust optimization techniques to handle the inherent energy consumption uncertainty over multiple periods.

## **4\. Exact Methodologies: Branch-Price-and-Cut (BPC)**

Given the NP-hard nature of the PTP and the structural weakness of the standard MILP formulations, solving these models to proven optimality requires sophisticated polyhedral techniques. The undisputed state-of-the-art exact solver architecture for the PTP and MPTP relies on Dantzig-Wolfe decomposition, implemented within a Branch-Price-and-Cut (BPC) framework.14

### **4.1. Set Partitioning Formulation**

Dantzig-Wolfe decomposition reformulates the edge-based MILP into a path-based Set Partitioning (or Set Packing) problem, transferring the complex routing constraints into the column generation subproblem.

Let ![][image53] be the set of all feasible elementary paths (columns) generated by the fleet that satisfy capacity, time windows, and energy constraints. Let ![][image54] be the binary decision variable indicating if route ![][image55] is selected. Let ![][image56] if node ![][image23] is visited by route ![][image47], and ![][image9] otherwise. Let ![][image57] be the total profit collected on route ![][image47], and ![][image51] be the total cost of route ![][image47]. The Restricted Master Problem (RMP) is defined as:

![][image58]
Subject to:

![][image59]
![][image60]
![][image61]
By relaxing the integrality constraint on ![][image62] to ![][image63], we obtain the Linear Programming (LP) relaxation of the RMP. The dual variables associated with the node visitation constraints (![][image64]) and the fleet size constraint (![][image65]) are passed to the pricing subproblem.

### **4.2. Implementation of the Restricted Master Problem**

When developing solutions for venues like INFORMS or *Operations Research*, providing clear, reproducible computational frameworks is essential. Below is the Python implementation using gurobipy to formulate and iteratively solve the RMP. This code demonstrates the column generation loop architecture required to solve the MPTP.

Python

import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB
import numpy as np

def build\_rmp(nodes, K, initial\_routes):
    """
    Constructs the Restricted Master Problem for the Profitable Tour Problem.
    nodes: list of customer indices.
    K: maximum number of available vehicles.
    initial\_routes: list of dictionaries representing initial feasible columns.
                    Format: \[{'cost': float, 'profit': float, 'visits': \[node\_id,...\]}\]
    """
    model \= gp.Model("PTP\_Restricted\_Master\_Problem")
    model.Params.OutputFlag \= 0  \# Suppress output for iterative column generation

    \# Decision variables for routes (lambda)
    lambda\_vars \=
    for r\_idx, route in enumerate(initial\_routes):
        net\_profit \= route\['profit'\] \- route\['cost'\]
        \# Relaxed to continuous for the column generation phase
        var \= model.addVar(vtype=GRB.CONTINUOUS, lb=0, ub=1, obj=net\_profit, name=f"lambda\_{r\_idx}")
        lambda\_vars.append(var)

    model.ModelSense \= GRB.MAXIMIZE

    \# Node visitation constraints: each node is visited at most once
    visit\_constrs \= {}
    for i in nodes:
        expr \= gp.LinExpr()
        for r\_idx, route in enumerate(initial\_routes):
            if i in route\['visits'\]:
                expr.addTerms(1.0, lambda\_vars\[r\_idx\])
        visit\_constrs\[i\] \= model.addConstr(expr \<= 1.0, name=f"visit\_{i}")

    \# Fleet size constraint: use at most K vehicles
    fleet\_expr \= gp.LinExpr()
    for var in lambda\_vars:
        fleet\_expr.addTerms(1.0, var)
    fleet\_constr \= model.addConstr(fleet\_expr \<= K, name="fleet\_limit")

    return model, lambda\_vars, visit\_constrs, fleet\_constr

def extract\_duals(model, visit\_constrs, fleet\_constr):
    """
    Optimizes the RMP and extracts dual variables for the pricing subproblem.
    """
    model.optimize()

    if model.Status\!= GRB.OPTIMAL:
        raise Exception("RMP did not solve to optimality.")

    duals\_nodes \= {i: constr.Pi for i, constr in visit\_constrs.items()}
    dual\_fleet \= fleet\_constr.Pi

    return duals\_nodes, dual\_fleet

**Computational Limitations and Tuning:** The RMP initialization must be handled carefully. If initial\_routes does not contain a diverse set of feasible paths, the dual variables generated will be highly erratic, causing the pricing problem to search unpromising areas of the state space. It is standard practice to initialize the RMP using a fast heuristic (like ALNS) and to apply dual stabilization techniques (e.g., trust regions or smoothing) to prevent extreme oscillations in ![][image66] during the early column generation iterations.

### **4.3. The Pricing Subproblem: ESPPRC and ng-Path Relaxations**

The pricing subproblem identifies columns (routes) with a positive reduced profit to insert into the RMP. For the PTP, this takes the form of an Elementary Shortest Path Problem with Resource Constraints (ESPPRC), which is itself strongly NP-hard. The reduced cost of traversing an arc is calculated dynamically as ![][image67], where ![][image66] are the dual variables retrieved from the RMP's node visitation constraints.

To solve the ESPPRC, classical approaches use bounded dynamic programming (DP) via labeling algorithms.16 A label ![][image68] at node ![][image23] represents a partial path and stores resource consumptions (cost, time, load). However, guaranteeing *elementarity* (preventing nodes from being visited multiple times in the same path) requires storing the entire set of visited nodes in the label, leading to a catastrophic combinatorial explosion in the state space.

**Algorithmic Innovation and Audit:** The state-of-the-art mitigation strategy is the *ng-path relaxation*.17 Instead of tracking all historically visited nodes, the state only tracks a highly restricted subset of recently visited nearby nodes, denoted ![][image69] (the ng-set), for each node ![][image23]. A path is considered feasible if it contains no cycles within the localized neighborhoods defined by these ng-sets.

While the literature heavily relies on static ng-sets defined by geometric proximity, this methodology is deeply flawed when applied to profit-driven routing. In the PTP, a vehicle may bypass geographically close nodes if their profit ![][image15] is low, only to return to them later if the dual variable ![][image66] fluctuates. Static ng-sets fail to prevent these high-profit cycles effectively. A mathematically rigorous implementation must utilize *dynamic ng-path relaxation*, where the ng-sets are iteratively expanded during the DP execution based on the specific cycles detected in the pricing solutions. In the context of the MPTP-EV, solving the subproblem with an exact approach utilizing purely elementary paths is heavily bottlenecked; leveraging dynamically adjusted ng-path relaxations has proven critical in allowing algorithms to process instances of up to 100 clients over 3 periods.3

## **5\. Advanced Matheuristics and Structural Metaheuristics**

Given the NP-hard nature of the ESPPRC, exact methods inevitably falter on large-scale instances (e.g., ![][image70]). Top-tier routing solutions currently deploy sophisticated hybrid algorithms, blending global metaheuristics with exact mixed-integer programming, creating *matheuristics*.18

### **5.1. Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS)**

ALNS is the dominant heuristic framework for the PTP and its highly constrained variants, such as the Multi-Vehicle PTP with Flexible Compartments and Mandatory Customers (MVPTPFC-MC).19 ALNS navigates the search space by iteratively applying a diverse set of *destroy* and *repair* operators.

In the context of the PTP, ALNS must be distinctly modified from its standard CVRP implementations:

* **Adaptivity and Dynamic Weighting:** The algorithm evaluates operator performance continuously. Operator weights (![][image71]) and selection probabilities (![][image72]) are updated dynamically via a roulette wheel mechanism using a reaction factor (![][image73]). If an operator successfully discovers a new global best solution, its weight is drastically increased.19
* **Unvisited Lists and Marginal Profit Evaluation:** Because the PTP does not mandate full coverage, the framework must maintain a dynamic list of *unvisited optional customers*. Repair operators must be mathematically designed to evaluate the marginal profit-to-cost ratio of inserting an optional customer from this pool versus removing an existing one. Greedy insertion based purely on distance will fail; the operator must evaluate ![][image74].
* **Feasibility Preservation:** In variants with mandatory customers, the repair operators strictly prioritize the insertion of mandatory nodes before evaluating the profitability of optional nodes, ensuring feasibility is maintained before attempting objective optimization.19

**Audit of ALNS Rigor:**

While empirically successful, ALNS lacks formal asymptotic convergence guarantees. It is entirely possible for the algorithm to stall in deep local optima if the temperature decay schedule in the Simulated Annealing (SA) acceptance criterion is poorly calibrated. To ensure academic rigor, papers must benchmark the ALNS against exact lower bounds derived from the LP relaxation of the Set Partitioning formulation to explicitly quantify the optimality gap, rather than merely comparing against legacy heuristics.

### **5.2. Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Randomized Variable Neighborhood Descent**

For multi-period variants and the PTP with Simultaneous Pickup and Delivery (PTPSPD), high-performance frameworks integrate cluster-first, route-second heuristics with Iterated Local Search (ILS).4 The state-of-the-art methodology intensifies the search using a Randomized Variable Neighborhood Descent (RVND) strategy, dynamically cycling through varying neighborhood structures (e.g., 2-opt, Or-opt, cross-exchange).

Global exploration is promoted via random perturbation mechanisms regulated by adaptive threshold acceptance criteria. A dynamic tabu list further promotes diversification and prevents the search trajectory from falling into infinite loops. This specific algorithmic combination recently established new best-known lower bounds for 64 standard benchmark instances, validating the mathematical robustness of integrating randomized components within deep local search loops.20

## **6\. Deep Reinforcement Learning and Neural Combinatorial Optimization**

The most disruptive recent advancement in optimizing the PTP is the application of Artificial Intelligence, specifically Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL).21 Classical heuristics like ALNS require meticulous, problem-specific hyperparameter tuning and extensive domain expertise. NCO aims to automate rule discovery and generalize across broad distributions of problem instances.

### **6.1. DRL-ED-TSPP: Transformer-Based Architectures**

A leading approach to the PTP is the Deep Reinforcement Learning Encoder-Decoder model (DRL-ED-TSPP).21 This architecture discards traditional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in favor of attention mechanisms.

**Architecture Mechanics:**

1. **Composite Feature Embedding (CFE):** The model fuses heterogeneous inputs—such as spatial coordinates ![][image75], specific travel costs ![][image12], and node profits ![][image15]—into a unified, high-dimensional semantic space.
2. **Transformer-Based Encoder:** The architecture relies on stacked self-attention layers. This attention mechanism is mathematically superior at capturing the long-range spatio-temporal dependencies inherent in large routing graphs. It allows the model to dynamically compute attention weights ![][image76] that evaluate the relative profitability of distant node clusters, effectively predicting which geographic areas offer the highest profit density.24
3. **Two-Stage Attention Decoder:** The decoder operates via a dual mechanism. A multi-head global context layer models the overall route structure, while a single-head prioritization mechanism hones in on maximizing the profit (e.g., cultural value in tourism applications).21
4. **Dynamic Masking:** To enforce the structural constraints of the PTP (e.g., preventing sub-tours and ensuring capacity is respected), a dynamic masking strategy strictly sets the logits of infeasible actions to ![][image77] prior to the softmax policy distribution step.

Evaluations of the DRL-ED-TSPP against state-of-the-art baselines show reductions in inference times by 9–15%, alongside 4.6–7.0% improvements in cost-to-value ratios.21

**Audit of DRL Generalization:**

Despite these metrics, DRL models are notoriously fragile regarding distribution shifts. Models trained on uniform random spatial distributions frequently fail catastrophically when evaluated on highly clustered, real-world topologies or when the scale shifts from ![][image78] to ![][image79]. Generalization remains a critical vulnerability that top-tier research must address through meta-learning or contrastive representation learning.

### **6.2. AlphaZero and Online Stochastic Optimization**

The PTP becomes substantially more difficult under stochasticity, such as the Online Stochastic PTP, where customer profits are treated as time-dependent random variables whose exact realizations only manifest sequentially online.22 Traditional a priori optimization fails entirely here. Recent studies have adapted the AlphaZero framework—originally designed for adversarial games—to this routing variant.22 By leveraging Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) guided by deep neural networks evaluating state-value functions ![][image80] and policy distributions ![][image81], the algorithm can simulate trajectories into possible stochastic realizations. The training via self-play effectively distills a highly robust, reactive policy that updates routing decisions online without computationally prohibitive re-optimizations.22

### **6.3. DeCoST: Handling Continuous Variables in Routing**

Many multi-period PTP variants involve continuous variables, such as service-time constraints or flexible time windows (OPTWVP). Discrete-action DRL models struggle natively with continuous decision spaces, often requiring arbitrary discretization that degrades solution quality. The newly introduced DeCoST (DEcoupled discrete-Continuous optimization with Service-time-guided Trajectory) model resolves this structural limitation.26 It employs a two-stage approach: a learned graph-transformer policy constructs the initial discrete sequence (the routing order), followed by a polynomial-time algorithm that optimally resolves the continuous variables (arrival and specific service durations) for that specific fixed route. This decoupling strategy represents a massive mathematical advantage over attempting to learn continuous time-displacements directly through policy gradients.

## **7\. Neural Column Generation (NCG)**

Perhaps the most potent convergence of AI and Operations Research is *Neural Column Generation*.23 While BPC provides exact solutions, solving the ESPPRC subproblem repeatedly is the primary bottleneck. NCG utilizes Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) or Graph Attention Networks (GATs) to predict the likelihood of specific columns (routes) or edges appearing in the optimal integer solution.

By applying a Universal Constrained Preference Optimization (UCPO) framework, the GNN learns a preference-based objective incorporating feasibility margins and primal refinements.28 In practice, this serves as a highly intelligent *partial column generation* heuristic: the neural network prunes the graph by discarding low-probability edges before the dynamic programming algorithm runs, vastly shrinking the state space. Empirical data shows this reduces computing time by up to 30%, particularly on hard instances evaluated under tight operational time limits.29

### **7.1. Implementation of a Neural Edge Evaluator**

To successfully implement NCG, a neural architecture must be designed to evaluate the graph edges based on the dual variables passed from the RMP. Below is a PyTorch implementation of a Graph Attention layer tailored for predicting edge probabilities in the PTP pricing subproblem.

Python

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class EdgeEvaluatorGAT(nn.Module):
    """
    A Graph Attention Network module designed to predict the probability
    that an edge belongs to an optimal column in the PTP pricing subproblem.
    """
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, node\_feature\_dim, edge\_feature\_dim, hidden\_dim):
        super(EdgeEvaluatorGAT, self).\_\_init\_\_()
        \# Project node features (e.g., coordinates, profit, demand)
        self.node\_proj \= nn.Linear(node\_feature\_dim, hidden\_dim)
        \# Project edge features (e.g., routing cost, dual variable pi\_i)
        self.edge\_proj \= nn.Linear(edge\_feature\_dim, hidden\_dim)

        \# Attention mechanism weights
        self.attn\_weight \= nn.Linear(3 \* hidden\_dim, 1)

        \# Edge classification head (probability of inclusion)
        self.edge\_classifier \= nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(3 \* hidden\_dim, hidden\_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden\_dim, 1),
            nn.Sigmoid()
        )

    def forward(self, node\_feats, edge\_feats, edge\_index):
        """
        node\_feats: Tensor of shape \[N, node\_feature\_dim\]
        edge\_feats: Tensor of shape \[E, edge\_feature\_dim\] (Must include dual variables)
        edge\_index: Tensor of shape \[2, E\] representing (source, target)
        """
        \# Project features to hidden dimension
        h\_nodes \= self.node\_proj(node\_feats)
        h\_edges \= self.edge\_proj(edge\_feats)

        src, dst \= edge\_index, edge\_index

        \# Concatenate source node, target node, and edge features
        \# Shape: \[E, 3 \* hidden\_dim\]
        edge\_context \= torch.cat(\[h\_nodes\[src\], h\_nodes\[dst\], h\_edges\], dim=-1)

        \# Compute attention coefficients (LeakyReLU standard for GATs)
        attn\_scores \= F.leaky\_relu(self.attn\_weight(edge\_context))

        \# Predict edge inclusion probability based on the context
        edge\_probs \= self.edge\_classifier(edge\_context)

        return edge\_probs, attn\_scores

**Training and Validation Instructions:**

To train this model, an offline dataset must be generated using an exact DP solver on small to medium instances. The ground truth labels ![][image82] indicate whether an edge was part of a column that ultimately formed the optimal integer solution of the RMP. The network is trained using Binary Cross-Entropy loss. During inference within the BPC solver, the RMP passes the updated dual variables ![][image66] into the edge\_feats tensor. Edges where edge\_probs \< threshold are temporarily pruned from the graph before the labeling algorithm executes.

**Critical Audit of NCG:**

While NCG dramatically accelerates the pricing problem, relying on it entirely compromises the exactness of the BPC algorithm. If the neural network incorrectly prunes an edge that belongs to a column with negative reduced cost, the column generation process will terminate prematurely, yielding a suboptimal lower bound. A rigorous algorithm must use NCG for the initial fast iterations of column generation, but systematically reintroduce the pruned edges and execute standard DP in the final iterations to mathematically guarantee that no valid columns remain undiscovered.

## **8\. Emerging Paradigms: Quantum Computing**

While deep learning tackles the heuristic bounds, early research into Quantum Computing attempts to shift the fundamental computational complexity of the PTP. The Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) has been applied to the PTP as a proof-of-concept for near-term quantum hardware.30

To apply VQE, the classical objective function must be mapped into a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model, which is then translated into a quantum Hamiltonian ![][image83]. The binary decision variables ![][image84] are transformed using Pauli-Z operators (![][image85]). The ground state of this Hamiltonian, which minimizes the expectation value ![][image86], corresponds to the optimal routing solution.30

The VQE algorithm utilizes parameterized quantum circuits to prepare the trial state ![][image87], while a classical optimizer (such as COBYLA or SPSA) iteratively updates the parameters ![][image88] to minimize the energy landscape. While currently limited by hardware noise in the NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) era and severe qubit scaling limitations, the novel methodology for constructing constraint-aware variational ansatzes presents a frontier methodology. The ability to map complex profit-routing tradeoffs into energy landscapes will eventually allow quantum heuristics to evaluate combinatorially vast subsets of customers simultaneously.30

## **9\. Strategic Research Directions and Existing Gaps**

Despite extensive progress in exact methods and NCO, several critical vulnerabilities and underexplored areas remain in the literature, presenting prime opportunities for researchers targeting top-tier AI and OR venues.

### **9.1. Robustness in Multi-Period and EV Routing**

**The Gap:** Current formulations of the MPTP-EV 3 rely heavily on deterministic approximations of battery consumption and charging times. However, the energy consumption of EVs is profoundly stochastic. **The Direction:** The community must pivot toward Robust Optimization (RO) frameworks or Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) for the MPTP. Integrating time-dependent travel velocities and non-linear energy discharge curves into the pricing subproblem of a BPC algorithm remains an open, highly complex mathematical challenge. Researchers should explore dynamically adjusting ng-path definitions based on the accumulated variance in the SoC tracker to prevent the DP from exploring inherently risky paths.

### **9.2. Complex Compartmentalization in HRDS**

**The Gap:** The Multi-Vehicle PTP with Flexible Compartments (MVPTPFC-MC) 19 models discrete compartmental boundaries but assumes basic 1D volumetric limitations. **The Direction:** In Home-Replenishment Delivery Systems (HRDS) and multi-temperature cold chain logistics, constraints such as Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) or 2D/3D packing orientations strictly interact with routing.23 Extending the ALNS or BPC algorithms to solve the PTP combined with 2D-Bin Packing constraints requires novel subproblem formulations. The integration of Neural Column generation specifically tailored for 2L-CVRP (Two-Dimensional Loading constraints) 28 integrated with profit maximization presents an open, highly publishable niche for venues like NeurIPS or IJCAI.

### **9.3. Theoretical Approximability and the TSP-T3CO Scheme**

**The Gap:** While heuristic performance is actively benchmarked using computational time and gap-to-best metrics, the formal theoretical approximability bounds of highly constrained PTP variants (such as the MPTP with flexible time windows) remain entirely opaque. **The Direction:** Leveraging the TSP-T3CO definition scheme 2, future mathematical analyses should seek to prove strict inapproximability bounds for the MPTP. Developing Polynomial-Time Approximation Schemes (PTAS) for the MPTP on restricted graph topologies (e.g., planar graphs, bounded treewidth networks) will provide fundamental theoretical value that purely empirical NCO papers lack.7 Establishing rigorous bounds on the loss of performance due to spatial clustering heuristics is essential for moving the field forward.

### **9.4. Unified NCO Baselines and Zero-Shot Generalization**

**The Gap:** DRL models like DRL-ED-TSPP 21 and DeCoST 26 often overfit to specific spatial distributions. They fail to scale effectively and require costly retraining when deployed in entirely new geographic regions. **The Direction:** Research must aggressively pursue "zero-shot" generalization in NCO. Techniques such as Meta-Learning, continuous curriculum learning, and contrastive representation learning should be applied to the PTP graph embeddings to make the neural networks agnostic to graph size and specific coordinate distributions. If a DRL agent can successfully route a multi-period PTP without retraining when the graph size shifts dramatically, it will effectively displace classical matheuristics in commercial routing software. The focus must shift from achieving marginal percentage gains on static benchmarks to proving model robustness across wildly divergent distribution shifts.

## **10\. Synthesis and Strategic Outlook**

The Profitable Tour Problem and its multi-period extensions are undergoing a period of profound mathematical and algorithmic evolution. The transition from static, single-objective models to dynamic, multi-period frameworks is largely dictated by the operational demands of electric vehicle logistics, stochastic tourism demands, and the necessity of sustainable supply chains. From an exact methodology standpoint, Branch-Price-and-Cut, augmented by dynamic ng-path relaxations and problem-specific capacity-like cuts, remains the absolute standard for establishing mathematical optimality and auditing heuristic bounds.

However, the absolute vanguard of the field lies in the hybridization of Operations Research and Artificial Intelligence. The utilization of Transformer-based reinforcement learning for dynamic route masking, the decoupling of continuous variables via DeCoST, and the application of Graph Attention Networks for precise Neural Column Generation represent a paradigm shift. Future breakthrough papers targeted at NeurIPS, ICLR, or *Operations Research* must abandon pure, mathematically ungrounded metaheuristics in favor of these unified, theoretically robust architectures. By directly addressing non-linear operational constraints through Robust Optimization, and embedding algorithmic exactness within AI-driven column prediction, researchers can solve the fundamental tension between computational scalability and mathematical rigor in the Multi-Period Profitable Tour Problem.
